# LiftOn vs Liftoff vs miniprot — Benchmark Comparison

Three-way comparison of standalone **Liftoff**, standalone **miniprot**, and **LiftOn** (reusing both via `-L`/`-M`) across 4 chromosome-subset benchmarks. Primary metrics from a custom evaluator reusing LiftOn's own parasail kernel (`align.py` + `get_id_fraction.py`); cross-checked with `liftofftools variants`. Benchmarks: Human GRCh38 -> CHM13 (MANE), D. melanogaster -> D. erecta, Arabidopsis TAIR10 -> ASM2311539v1, Rice IRGSP -> ASM3414082v1.

> **Reading the metrics.** *Completeness (coding)* = recovered coding transcripts / reference coding transcripts. *Protein identity* is CDS-translated and directly comparable across tools. *DNA identity* basis is full-transcript (exon-spliced) for Liftoff/LiftOn but CDS-spliced for miniprot (it has no UTR/exon records) — see the `DNA basis` column. Cross-species benchmarks subset both genomes to one syntenic chromosome, so non-syntenic reference genes count as lost by design.

## 1. Master comparison

| Benchmark | Tool | Completeness (coding) | Mean prot id | Median prot id | % identical | Mean DNA id | Wall (s) | Peak RSS (MB) | DNA basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | liftoff | 99.8% | 0.993 | 1.000 | 72.3% | 0.998 | 15.3 | 553 | transcript |
| human_mane | miniprot | 100.0% | 0.990 | 1.000 | 61.0% | 0.995 | 2.3 | 722 | cds |
| human_mane | lifton | 99.8% | 0.994 | 1.000 | 72.3% | 0.998 | 14.4 | 2307 | transcript |
| human_mane | lifton_optimize | 99.8% | 0.994 | 1.000 | 72.3% | 0.998 | 14.4 | 2178 | transcript |
| drosophila | liftoff | 98.2% | 0.908 | 0.958 | 4.8% | 0.890 | 118.0 | 3127 | transcript |
| drosophila | miniprot | 99.0% | 0.921 | 0.958 | 4.1% | 0.919 | 6.6 | 431 | cds |
| drosophila | lifton | 98.2% | 0.925 | 0.958 | 4.8% | 0.890 | 148.2 | 4825 | transcript |
| drosophila | lifton_optimize | 98.3% | 0.926 | 0.958 | 4.8% | 0.890 | 187.4 | 5079 | transcript |
| arabidopsis | liftoff | 99.8% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 68.8 | 2281 | transcript |
| arabidopsis | miniprot | 100.0% | 0.996 | 1.000 | 92.3% | 0.997 | 8.0 | 402 | cds |
| arabidopsis | lifton | 99.8% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 233.5 | 5086 | transcript |
| arabidopsis | lifton_optimize | 99.8% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 230.3 | 5032 | transcript |
| rice | liftoff | 100.0% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 27.9 | 927 | transcript |
| rice | miniprot | 100.0% | 0.995 | 1.000 | 91.6% | 0.998 | 4.3 | 606 | cds |
| rice | lifton | 100.0% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 116.1 | 3253 | transcript |
| rice | lifton_optimize | 100.0% | 0.999 | 1.000 | 99.5% | 1.000 | 109.1 | 3085 | transcript |


## 1b. All-feature-type completeness

Beyond coding transcripts, this tracks recovery of **every** reference feature type (genes, mRNAs, ncRNAs, tRNAs, rRNAs, lncRNAs, pseudogenes, …) by id. LiftOn inherits Liftoff's full-annotation lift, so the two track closely; miniprot is protein-only (emits its own ids) and is excluded from id-recovery here — its coding recovery is in the master table above.

| Benchmark | Liftoff all-feature compl. | LiftOn all-feature compl. | Δ (LiftOn−Liftoff) | Ref features |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | 99.8% | 99.8% | +0.0% | 846 |
| drosophila | 90.8% | 90.8% | +0.0% | 13157 |
| arabidopsis | 91.6% | 91.6% | +0.0% | 25276 |
| rice | 97.6% | 97.6% | +0.0% | 11905 |


<img alt="all-feature completeness" src="data:image/png;base64," style="max-width:100%;">


**Per-feature-type detail — MAIN benchmark (`human_mane`):**

| Feature type | Ref n | Liftoff recovered | Liftoff % | LiftOn recovered | LiftOn % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| gene | 423 | 422 | 99.8% | 422 | 99.8% |
| mRNA | 423 | 422 | 99.8% | 422 | 99.8% |


## 2. Head-to-head — does LiftOn improve on Liftoff?

Positive Δ = LiftOn better. The thesis: LiftOn ≥ Liftoff on protein identity and completeness by fusing miniprot's protein evidence.

| Benchmark | LiftOn compl. | Δ vs Liftoff | LiftOn mean prot id | Δ vs Liftoff | Δ vs miniprot |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | 99.8% | +0.0% | 0.994 | +0.001 | +0.005 |
| drosophila | 98.2% | +0.0% | 0.925 | +0.016 | +0.004 |
| arabidopsis | 99.8% | +0.0% | 0.999 | +0.000 | +0.003 |
| rice | 100.0% | +0.0% | 0.999 | +0.000 | +0.004 |


## 2b. LiftOn default vs `--optimize` (per-transcript protein-identity delta)

The opt-in `--optimize` lane runs a best-of-outcome Liftoff↔miniprot merge (keeps, per locus, whichever of Liftoff+ORF-rescue vs merge+ORF-rescue scores higher) plus a `update_cds_list` multi-exon corruption fix. Positive Δ = optimize better. The merge is **double-edged** — it rescues some transcripts hugely but can backfire on others — so every per-transcript regression is listed explicitly below, not hidden in the aggregate. (Deltas computed by re-aligning the emitted protein, independent of LiftOn's internal score.)

| Benchmark | Mean prot id (default) | Mean (optimize) | Δ mean | n common | improved | regressed | mean Δ/transcript |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | 0.994 | 0.994 | +0.000 | 422 | 0 | 0 | +0.00000 |
| drosophila | 0.925 | 0.926 | +0.001 | 7111 | 119 | 4 | +0.00166 |
| arabidopsis | 0.999 | 0.999 | +0.000 | 12632 | 2 | 1 | +0.00001 |
| rice | 0.999 | 0.999 | +0.000 | 5850 | 0 | 0 | +0.00000 |

**drosophila** — top improvements + all regressions:
  - +0.5859  `rna-NM_206523.3`  (0.2273 → 0.8131)
  - +0.4803  `rna-NM_169440.3`  (0.4873 → 0.9676)
  - +0.4700  `rna-NM_142162.3`  (0.1613 → 0.6313)
  - +0.4554  `rna-NM_170478.3`  (0.2079 → 0.6634)
  - +0.4033  `rna-NM_001300603.1`  (0.3567 → 0.7600)
  - **-0.0072  `rna-NM_001032040.3`  (0.2959 → 0.2888)  ← regression**
  - **-0.0072  `rna-NM_001347743.1`  (0.2959 → 0.2888)  ← regression**
  - **-0.0040  `rna-NM_142897.2`  (0.8469 → 0.8429)  ← regression**
  - **-0.0025  `rna-NM_001316572.1`  (0.9569 → 0.9543)  ← regression**

**arabidopsis** — top improvements + all regressions:
  - +0.0990  `rna-NM_106322.4`  (0.8557 → 0.9547)
  - +0.0963  `rna-NM_001334742.1`  (0.8596 → 0.9559)
  - **-0.0061  `rna-NM_001334286.1`  (0.9858 → 0.9798)  ← regression**

## 4. LiftoffTools cross-check

Independent confirmation on the Liftoff/LiftOn outputs (miniprot excluded — no gene hierarchy). `identical` count should track the custom evaluator's %identical × recovered.

| Benchmark | Tool | LiftoffTools identical | LiftoffTools n | Custom %identical×recovered | available |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | liftoff | 223 | 423 | 305 | yes |
| human_mane | lifton | 222 | 423 | 305 | yes |
| drosophila | liftoff | 6 | 8281 | 339 | yes |
| drosophila | lifton | 6 | 8279 | 339 | yes |
| arabidopsis | liftoff | — | — | 12573 | no |
| arabidopsis | lifton | 12568 | 15543 | 12573 | yes |
| rice | liftoff | — | — | 5823 | no |
| rice | lifton | 5825 | 7302 | 5823 | yes |


## 5. Pipeline

Reproduce with `python -m benchmarks.compare.run_compare --all -t 8` (resumable). Per benchmark: subset both genomes to one chromosome → run Liftoff and miniprot standalone → run LiftOn reusing both (`-L`/`-M`) → evaluate each output → cross-check with LiftoffTools → aggregate. Code under `benchmarks/compare/`.


## 6. Speed: serial vs threaded (byte-identical)

LiftOn run twice on the same cached `-L`/`-M` inputs (genes mode, non-`--optimize`): **serial** `-t 1` (no fast path) vs **threaded** `-t 8 --native --locus-pipeline` (the Phase-17b/17c per-locus materialised path). `Byte-identical` MUST be ✓ — this is the harness-side echo of the 24-cell matrix gate: the fast path is a wall-clock optimisation, never an output change. Reproduce with `python -m benchmarks.compare.speed_ab human_mane drosophila arabidopsis rice`.

| Dataset | Serial wall (s) | Threaded wall (s) | Speedup | Serial RSS (MB) | Threaded RSS (MB) | Byte-identical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| human_mane | 24.35 | 13.77 | 1.77× | 1516 | 2280 | ✓ |
| drosophila | 262.04 | 137.69 | 1.90× | 2520 | 4798 | ✓ |
| arabidopsis | 244.05 | 231.29 | 1.06× | 1558 | 5057 | ✓ |
| rice | 135.85 | 111.44 | 1.22× | 1474 | 3162 | ✓ |

**Verdict:** every dataset is byte-identical (hard gate passed) and threaded is faster than serial (1.06×–1.90×). The win scales with how much of wall-clock lives in Step 7's per-locus loop: drosophila (8.3K coding loci) gets 1.90×, while arabidopsis — where non-Step-7 work (DB build, miniprot ingest, large-feature serialisation) dominates — gets only 1.06×. The threaded path trades memory for time: peak RSS rises 1.5×–3.2× (the parent thread pre-fetches/materialises proxy DBs and holds more concurrent per-locus buffers), most visibly on arabidopsis (1558 → 5057 MB). Use `--native --locus-pipeline -t N` to cut wall-clock on Step-7-bound datasets; expect higher peak memory.
